one_line_takeaway: ATBench is a 1,000-trajectory, taxonomy-guided synthetic benchmark for long-horizon agent safety that cleanly separates 'is this trajectory unsafe?' from 'why?', showing frontier judges detect unsafe traces moderately well (GPT-5.4 76.7% F1) but fail badly at fine-grained causal attribution (33.6% risk source).
key_finding: ATBench is far from saturated even for frontier evaluators. On binary safe/unsafe classification the best F1 is GPT-5.4 at 76.7%, followed by Gemini-3.1-Pro (75.0%) and the trajectory-trained guard AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (71.1%). Fine-grained causal diagnosis is dramatically harder: GPT-5.4 reaches only 33.6% on risk source, 13.5% on failure mode, and 30.2% on real-world harm; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B is the strongest here (46.8% / 16.5% / 40.6%). Recognizing that a trajectory is unsafe is far easier than reconstructing where the risk originated, how it unfolded, and what harm it caused. Difficulty is concentrated in tool-interface attribution and tool-centered failure mechanisms: e.g., the 'choosing malicious tool' failure mode reaches only 16.7% for GPT-5.4 and 5.6% for AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B, and malicious tool execution is near zero for most evaluators. Cross-benchmark comparison shows most models drop sharply on ATBench versus prior suites (GPT-5.2: 90.8% on R-Judge vs 69.0% here; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B: 91.8% vs 64.0%), which the authors attribute to broader tool coverage, longer trajectories, and delayed-trigger construction rather than synthetic noise.
core_contribution: ATBench is a trajectory-level benchmark for evaluating the safety of LLM-based agents over complete multi-turn interaction traces (user requests, agent thoughts, tool calls, and environment feedback) rather than isolated prompts or final answers. Its own contributions are: (1) a 1,000-trajectory dataset (503 safe, 497 unsafe) built from a heterogeneous tool pool; (2) a three-dimensional safety taxonomy over risk source (8 leaf categories), failure mode (14), and real-world harm (10) that doubles as a coverage-control scaffold during construction and as a fine-grained diagnostic label space (note: the paper states this taxonomy follows the authors' earlier AgentDoG work, so the taxonomy structure is adapted rather than wholly novel); (3) a taxonomy-guided data generation engine combining planner-based trajectory synthesis, paired safe/unsafe construction, and a setup-exploit 'delayed-trigger' long-context protocol where a risk is planted early and realized later; and (4) an evaluation protocol splitting binary safe/unsafe classification (ATBench-Coarse) from fine-grained causal diagnosis (ATBench-Fine) over the three taxonomy axes. Prior trajectory benchmarks (R-Judge, AgentSafetyBench, AgentAuditor/ASSEBench) and tool-pool sources (ToolBench, ToolAlpaca, ToolACE, RapidAPI) are cited work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The benchmark models agentic safety risks that emerge gradually across long-horizon, tool-mediated interactions rather than from single prompts. Its risk-source axis enumerates the adversarial and non-adversarial entry points it covers: malicious user instruction/jailbreak and direct prompt injection (user input); indirect prompt injection and unreliable/misinformation (environmental observation); tool description injection, malicious tool execution, and corrupted tool feedback (external tools/APIs); and inherent agent/LLM failures (internal logic). A central threat pattern is the delayed-trigger 'setup-exploit' scenario, where earlier benign-appearing actions or acquired permissions become safety-critical later in the trajectory. ATBench is an evaluation testbed, not a defense: the 'adversary' is embodied in the synthesized unsafe trajectories, and the 'defender' under test is a judge/guard model tasked with detecting the unsafe trajectory and attributing its risk source, failure mode, and harm.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) each unsafe trajectory carries only one primary label per taxonomy dimension, potentially missing valid secondary interpretations in multi-causal cases; (2) all trajectories are English-only, limiting multilingual applicability; (3) the benchmark covers only text-and-tool trajectories, excluding multimodal and embodied settings. Reviewer-observed: the dataset is entirely synthetically generated by LLM-based simulators (query, tool call, tool feedback, and agent response are all model-produced), so 'realism' rests on the filtering and human-audit passes rather than on real deployment traces; the three-dimensional taxonomy and the AgentDoG prompt template are inherited from the authors' own prior AgentDoG work, and AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (same group) is both a baseline and the top fine-grained performer, which is worth noting when interpreting the leaderboard. Some fine-grained leaf categories have very small support (e.g., n=3 for harmful/offensive content, n=10 for malicious executables), making per-category accuracies noisy.

datasets_used: ATBench (this paper's benchmark): 1,000 trajectories, 503 safe / 497 unsafe, avg 9.01 turns, avg 3.95k tokens, 1,954 invoked tools from a pool of 2,084 available tools
ATBench500 (earlier 500-case release of the same benchmark, used as a comparison point): 500 cases, 1,575 available tools, 1,357 used tools, 8.97 avg turns, 1.52k avg tokens

Tool pool sourced/adapted from ToolBench (ToolLLM), ToolAlpaca, ToolACE, and real-world public APIs (e.g., RapidAPI), then normalized, deduplicated, and expanded with simulated tools (10,000+ API schemas in the generation engine)
Cross-benchmark comparison against prior trajectory-safety benchmarks: R-Judge (564 cases, 148 used tools), ASSE-Safety (1,476 cases, 903 used tools), ASSE-Security (817 cases, 254 used tools)
quantitative_results: Binary classification F1 (unsafe = positive): GPT-5.4 76.7% (Acc 73.7, Prec 68.5, Rec 87.1), Gemini-3.1-Pro 75.0%, Gemini-3-Flash 74.9%, GPT-5.2 71.8%, AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B 71.1%, ShieldAgent 67.7%, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B 67.8%, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 61.9%, Qwen3-235B-A22B 60.8%
Guard models weak on binary recall: LlamaGuard3-8B F1 7.3% (Rec 3.8), Qwen3-Guard F1 0.8% (Rec 0.4), LlamaGuard4-12B F1 41.7%

Fine-grained diagnosis accuracy (Risk Source / Failure Mode / Real-world Harm) over unsafe trajectories: GPT-5.4 33.6 / 13.5 / 30.2; GPT-5.2 29.5 / 12.0 / 26.8; Gemini-3.1-Pro 24.8 / 12.6 / 18.5; Qwen3.5-397B 7.7 / 3.6 / 6.8; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B (best) 46.8 / 16.5 / 40.6

Hard leaf categories: 'choosing malicious tool' failure mode 16.7% (GPT-5.4), 22.2% (Qwen3.5-397B), 5.6% (AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B); 'malicious tool execution' risk source 24.0% (AgentDoG) and near-zero for the other three evaluators; vs. tractable 'malicious user/jailbreak' risk source 73.8% (GPT-5.4), 83.6% (Qwen3.5-397B)
Cross-benchmark accuracy drop onto ATBench: GPT-5.2 90.8% (R-Judge) to 69.0% (ATBench); Qwen3-235B 85.1% to 59.2%; AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B 91.8% to 64.0%; QwQ-32B 89.5% to 57.7%
Human audit produced 5 binary corrections and 165 fine-grained corrections across 129 unsafe trajectories
baselines_compared: Closed-source judges: GPT-5.4, GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-3.1-Pro
Open-source general models: Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, Qwen3.5-4B, Qwen3-4B, QwQ-32B, Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct
Specialized guard models: LlamaGuard3-8B, LlamaGuard4-12B, Qwen3-Guard, ShieldAgent, AgentDoG-Qwen3-4B
